past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 15 years ago, managed with amlodipine
Coronary artery disease with prior percutaneous coronary intervention 6 years ago
Essential tremor diagnosed 8 years ago by primary care physician, treated intermittently with propranolol
Benign prostatic hyperplasia with urinary urgency
Unintentional weight loss of 6 kg over 4 months
family_history: Father died at age 80 of stroke, had essential tremor from age 60
Mother died at age 82 of congestive heart failure, no neurological disease
One brother, age 70, has essential tremor
No known family history of Parkinson's disease, dementia, or atypical parkinsonism

social_history.occupation: Retired mechanical engineer; stopped working 3 years ago
social_history.living_situation: Lives with his wife in a two-story home; considering moving due to difficulty with stairs
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 25 years ago, 15 pack-year history
social_history.alcohol: Rare, 1-2 glasses of wine per month; reduced due to balance concerns
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 2 miles daily; stopped 6 months ago due to fatigue and unsteadiness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, date, and situation. MMSE 27/30, losing points on serial sevens (2 errors) and recall (missed 1 of 3 words, retrieved with category cue). MoCA 24/30, with difficulty on trail-making (2 errors), verbal fluency (9 animals in 60 seconds), and delayed recall (2/5 words, retrieved 1 with cue). Speech mildly hypophonic with occasional slurring. No aphasia.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No vertical gaze palsy. Facial expression markedly hypomimic bilaterally. Mild bilateral jaw jerk. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk mildly reduced globally. Tone increased bilaterally with lead-pipe rigidity at wrists, elbows, and neck; cogwheel phenomenon difficult to elicit due to absence of concurrent tremor. Bradykinesia present bilaterally on finger tapping and hand opening/closing with early decrement and fatigue, approximately symmetric. No action or postural tremor observed during 10-minute examination. Only intermittent low-amplitude postural tremor of right hand with re-emergence after 15 seconds of sustained posture, frequency approximately 5-6 Hz. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Vibration mildly reduced at great toes bilaterally. Proprioception intact. No cortical sensory loss.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Babinski sign. Palmomental reflex present bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin mildly clumsy bilaterally, difficult to distinguish from bradykinesia versus mild cerebellar component. Rapid alternating movements moderately slowed bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Narrow to mildly wide-based gait with markedly reduced bilateral arm swing. Moderate postural instability on pull test — took 4 retropulsive steps on first attempt and required assistance on second attempt. Turning requires 6-7 steps with en bloc turning. No freezing of gait. Tandem walk impaired with lateral deviation. Romberg mildly positive.
neurological_exam.additional: Orthostatic vital signs: BP supine 148/90 mmHg, HR 68; BP standing at 1 minute 114/70 mmHg, HR 74; BP standing at 3 minutes 104/62 mmHg, HR 76. Patient reported lightheadedness and graying of vision on standing. Previous essential tremor diagnosis is questioned — the original tremor was reportedly bilateral postural and action tremor of the hands that responded partially to propranolol, but the current examination shows no clear action or postural tremor, only intermittent low-amplitude re-emergent postural tremor. Mild anterocollis noted. No myoclonus. No dystonia.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive slowing of movements, gait unsteadiness, recurrent near-falls, and worsening dizziness on standing over the past 16 months
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Heinrich Mayer is a 74-year-old right-handed retired mechanical engineer referred to the neurology movement disorders clinic for evaluation of progressive motor decline over the past sixteen months. He is accompanied by his wife, who provides substantial collateral history. The patient was originally diagnosed with essential tremor eight years ago by his primary care physician after presenting with bilateral hand tremor that was worse with action and partially responsive to propranolol. Over the past sixteen months, his wife has observed a gradual but relentless decline in his motor function that she considers qualitatively different from his long-standing tremor. He has become progressively slower in all activities of daily living — taking twice as long to dress, requiring assistance with buttons, and struggling with utensils during meals. His handwriting has become progressively smaller and illegible. His facial expression has diminished to the point that family members have asked whether he is depressed. Approximately ten months ago, he began experiencing episodes of lightheadedness and near-syncope upon standing, initially attributed to his antihypertensive medication. Amlodipine was reduced from 10 mg to 5 mg, then increased back to 10 mg when blood pressure remained elevated while supine, yet orthostatic symptoms persisted. He has had four near-falls and one complete fall while turning in the bathroom approximately three months ago, striking his shoulder. His wife reports that his balance difficulties seemed to appear relatively early in the course, within the first six months of symptom onset. Constipation has worsened despite daily polyethylene glycol, and he reports new urinary urgency with two episodes of urge incontinence in the past two months. He has lost approximately 6 kg over four months without dietary changes, attributing this to fatigue and reduced appetite. His wife notes that his snoring has worsened dramatically over the past year, and she has witnessed episodes where he appears to act out his dreams, on one occasion striking her while asleep. He also describes general muscular fatigue and proximal weakness, particularly when climbing stairs or rising from chairs, though he is unsure whether this represents true weakness or slowness. Notably, the tremor that was his original presenting symptom eight years ago appears to have changed in character or largely resolved; his wife reports that his hands no longer shake the way they used to. His primary care physician initiated a trial of carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 mg three times daily four months ago. After eight weeks with equivocal benefit — his wife estimates at most ten to fifteen percent subjective improvement in morning stiffness — the dose was increased to 25/200 mg three times daily for an additional six weeks with no further benefit. Jaw and joint pain have been intermittent complaints over several months, and he has experienced mild low-grade temperatures up to 37.8 degrees Celsius on a few occasions, though no clear infectious source has been identified.

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Subtle linear hyperintensity along the posterolateral margin of both putamina (putaminal rim sign)
signal_characteristics.SWI: Hypointensity in posterolateral putamina bilaterally, consistent with increased iron deposition

signal_characteristics.T2: Cruciform hyperintensity within the central pons (hot cross bun sign)

signal_characteristics.T1: Moderate cerebellar vermian and hemispheric volume loss, disproportionate to age

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Moderate periventricular and subcortical white matter hyperintensities, Fazekas grade 2

signal_characteristics.T2: Small area of gliosis, likely old lacunar infarct

signal_characteristics.T1: Midbrain morphology preserved, no hummingbird sign

volumetrics.putaminal_volume: Reduced bilaterally, approximately 8th percentile for age
volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Mildly reduced bilaterally, approximately 20th percentile for age

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No mass lesions or pathological enhancement post-gadolinium
No ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy
Middle cerebellar peduncles appear atrophic with subtle T2 signal change
Moderate small vessel ischemic disease — may contribute to but does not explain the parkinsonian syndrome
mri.impression: Brain MRI demonstrating findings concerning for an atypical parkinsonian syndrome. The combination of bilateral putaminal volume loss with putaminal rim sign, pontine hot cross bun sign, cerebellar and middle cerebellar peduncle atrophy raises high suspicion for multiple system atrophy (MSA) with both parkinsonian and cerebellar features. Moderate small vessel ischemic disease is present but insufficient to explain the clinical syndrome. An incidental old left cerebellar lacunar infarct is noted. No features of normal pressure hydrocephalus. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria
Nonspecific ST-T wave changes in lateral leads
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 66 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria with secondary repolarization changes. No arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or acute ischemic changes.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: BUN 22 mg/dL (H), ESR 32 mm/hr (H), CRP 0.8 mg/dL (H), Serum protein electrophoresis Monoclonal band in IgA region  (H), IgA 425 mg/dL (H).

signal_characteristics.pattern: Symmetric bilateral reduction in putaminal tracer uptake with early caudate involvement

additional_observations: Bilateral loss of normal comma-shaped striatal uptake, replaced by dot/period pattern
Early caudate involvement at this clinical stage raises concern for atypical parkinsonian syndrome
Degree of striatal loss correlates with severity of clinical bradykinesia
output.impression: ABNORMAL DaTscan demonstrating marked bilateral symmetric reduction in striatal dopamine transporter uptake, predominantly affecting the putamina with early caudate involvement. Clinical correlation recommended.

Levodopa_Challenge.value: Marked orthostatic hypotension (supine 150/88, standing 88/52 at 60 min), nausea, pre-syncopal episode requiring supine positioning

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Baseline UPDRS-III score 42 points (H), Post-levodopa UPDRS-III score (60 min) 39 points (H), Post-levodopa UPDRS-III score (90 min) 38 points (H), Levodopa response classification Poor  (H), Adverse effects Marked orthostatic hypotension (supine 150/88, standing 88/52 at 60 min), nausea, pre-syncopal episode requiring supine positioning  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Severe orthostatic hypotension provoked by levodopa challenge

Tilt_Table_Test.value: Absent late phase II recovery, absent phase IV overshoot

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Supine BP 150/92 mmHg (H), BP at 3 min upright tilt 92/56 mmHg (H), HR response to tilt 68 to 76 bpm (H), Valsalva ratio 1.08 ratio (L), Beat-to-beat BP during Valsalva Absent late phase II recovery, absent phase IV overshoot  (H), Forearm sweat output 0.38 µL/cm² (L), Proximal leg sweat output 0.22 µL/cm² (L), Distal leg sweat output 0.1 µL/cm² (L), Total body surface area anhidrosis 48% % (H), Post-void residual volume 185 mL (H), Detrusor overactivity Present  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Severe neurogenic orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop 58 mmHg)
Attenuated HR response to tilt (8 bpm increase)
Absent late phase II and phase IV on Valsalva
Reduced QSART sweat volumes at all sites

Polysomnography.value: 2 episodes of complex motor behavior during REM — patient sat up suddenly and appeared to defend against an unseen threat, vocalized
Inspiratory stridor detected during NREM stages 2-3 and during REM, recurring in episodes of 30-90 seconds

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Total sleep time 298 minutes (L), Sleep efficiency 64% % (H), REM without atonia (% of REM) 58% % (H), RBD episodes observed 2 episodes of complex motor behavior during REM — patient sat up suddenly and appeared to defend against an unseen threat, vocalized  (H), AHI 12.4 events/hr (H), Nocturnal stridor Inspiratory stridor detected during NREM stages 2-3 and during REM, recurring in episodes of 30-90 seconds  (H), Periodic limb movements index 28 events/hr (H).
abnormal_values_summary: 2 RBD episodes with complex motor behavior

additional_observations: Normal cardiac MIBG uptake with preserved heart-to-mediastinum ratios
output.impression: NORMAL cardiac MIBG scintigraphy with preserved postganglionic sympathetic cardiac innervation. In a patient with parkinsonism and severe autonomic failure, preserved cardiac MIBG uptake strongly favors multiple system atrophy over idiopathic Parkinson's disease. In PD, postganglionic cardiac sympathetic denervation typically results in reduced MIBG uptake, whereas in MSA the autonomic failure originates from preganglionic and central pathways with preservation of postganglionic cardiac neurons. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: multiple system atrophy vs Parkinson disease differentiation diagnostic criteria

results.title: Movement Disorder Society criteria for the diagnosis of multiple system atrophy
results.authors: Wenning GK, Stankovic I, Krismer F, et al.
results.summary: Updated MDS criteria introduce neuropathologically established, clinically established, and clinically probable MSA categories. Clinically established MSA requires: (1) sporadic progressive parkinsonism poorly responsive to levodopa OR cerebellar syndrome, (2) at least one autonomic dysfunction feature, (3) at least one supportive clinical or MRI feature. Supportive features include stridor, RBD, putaminal or cerebellar atrophy, and hot cross bun sign.

results.title: Distinguishing MSA from PD: clinical clues and investigational tools
results.summary: Key distinguishing features of MSA vs PD: symmetric onset, poor levodopa response, early severe autonomic failure, stridor, rapid progression, cerebellar signs, characteristic MRI changes (hot cross bun, putaminal rim), preserved cardiac MIBG in MSA vs reduced in PD, and symmetric DaTscan loss. Accuracy of clinical diagnosis during life remains approximately 62% for MSA.

results.title: Natural history and prognosis of multiple system atrophy
results.authors: Wenning GK, Geser F, Krismer F, et al.
results.summary: Mean survival 6-10 years from symptom onset. Stridor is associated with sudden death. Rate of progression is faster than PD. Key management is symptomatic: midodrine or droxidopa for orthostatic hypotension, nocturnal CPAP or tracheostomy for stridor.

output.summary: Current evidence supports differentiating MSA from PD based on: symmetric parkinsonism with poor levodopa response, early severe multi-domain autonomic failure, nocturnal stridor, characteristic MRI features (hot cross bun sign, putaminal rim sign, cerebellar/pontine atrophy), preserved cardiac MIBG uptake, and symmetric DaTscan pattern. This patient's presentation meets criteria for clinically established MSA per 2022 MDS guidelines.

output.proposed: Midodrine 5 mg three times daily for neurogenic orthostatic hypotension
interactions: Midodrine + Amlodipine: Pharmacological antagonism — midodrine is an alpha-1 agonist vasoconstrictor while amlodipine is a vasodilator. May partially offset each other's effects. Consider whether antihypertensive is still needed given orthostatic hypotension, or time doses to manage supine hypertension vs standing hypotension.

Midodrine + Tamsulosin: Significant interaction — tamsulosin is an alpha-1 antagonist that directly opposes midodrine's mechanism of action. Concurrent use will significantly reduce efficacy of midodrine for orthostatic hypotension.
contraindications: Midodrine is contraindicated in supine hypertension (patient has BP 148/88 supine) — risk of dangerous supine hypertension. Require careful timing: last dose no later than 4 hours before bedtime, and elevate head of bed 30 degrees.
warnings: Monitor supine blood pressure closely given existing supine hypertension
Urinary retention may worsen — patient already has elevated post-void residual if urodynamics performed
Scalp tingling and piloerection are common side effects
alternatives: Droxidopa (Northera) — norepinephrine prodrug, alternative for neurogenic orthostatic hypotension, less effect on supine BP
Fludrocortisone 0.1 mg daily — volume expansion; risk of supine hypertension and hypokalemia
Non-pharmacological measures: compression garments, increased salt and fluid intake, elevating head of bed

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: WAIS-IV Working Memory Index 84 standard score (L), WAIS-IV Processing Speed Index 74 standard score (L), CVLT-3 Long Delay Free Recall -1.5 z-score (H), Verbal Fluency (FAS) 24 total words (L), Trail Making Test A 52 seconds (H), Trail Making Test B 132 seconds (H), Stroop Color-Word Interference -2.0 z-score (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Trail Making Test A: 52 sec (mildly impaired) (H)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Multiple system atrophy, parkinsonian type with cerebellar features (MSA-P/MSA-C overlap)

differential.diagnosis: Multiple system atrophy (MSA-P with cerebellar features)
differential.key_features: Symmetric poorly levodopa-responsive parkinsonism, early severe multi-domain autonomic failure (neurogenic orthostatic hypotension, urogenital dysfunction), characteristic MRI changes (hot cross bun sign, putaminal rim sign, cerebellar and pontine atrophy), nocturnal stridor, RBD, preserved cardiac MIBG, symmetric DaTscan loss

The initial clinical framing and prior essential tremor diagnosis create a referral bias toward PD. However, symmetric onset, absent classic resting tremor, poor levodopa response, early severe autonomic failure, early postural instability, cerebellar/pontine MRI changes, and preserved cardiac MIBG all argue strongly against idiopathic PD

Early postural instability and falls are shared features. However, absence of vertical gaze palsy, preserved midbrain morphology (no hummingbird sign), presence of cerebellar atrophy, and autonomic failure pattern are not characteristic of PSP

Moderate white matter disease is present on MRI and the old cerebellar lacunar infarct could be considered relevant. However, the clinical pattern does not match lower-body vascular parkinsonism, and the MRI findings of putaminal rim sign and hot cross bun sign indicate a neurodegenerative rather than vascular etiology

Propranolol can theoretically worsen bradykinesia, but it does not cause the severe autonomic failure, structural brain changes, or progressive course seen in this patient. The degree of motor and autonomic impairment far exceeds what propranolol alone could produce

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination emphasizing symmetry of parkinsonism, tremor characteristics, postural stability, cerebellar signs, and orthostatic vital signs
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Symmetric bilateral rigidity and bradykinesia without classic resting tremor, early postural instability, possible subtle cerebellar signs, significant orthostatic hypotension with attenuated heart rate response

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to evaluate for structural causes and features of atypical parkinsonism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Putaminal atrophy with rim sign, pontine hot cross bun sign, cerebellar atrophy, and old cerebellar lacunar infarct

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory work-up including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, B12, ceruloplasmin, RPR, and inflammatory markers
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Largely normal with incidental monoclonal IgA gammopathy and mildly elevated inflammatory markers — no reversible cause of parkinsonism

optimal_actions.action: Request formal levodopa challenge test to assess dopaminergic responsiveness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Poor motor response (<30% UPDRS improvement) with provocation of severe orthostatic hypotension

optimal_actions.action: Request comprehensive autonomic function testing including tilt table, Valsalva, QSART, and thermoregulatory sweat test
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Severe multi-domain autonomic failure including neurogenic orthostatic hypotension, cardiovagal impairment, widespread sudomotor dysfunction, and neurogenic bladder

optimal_actions.action: Order DaTscan to confirm presynaptic dopaminergic denervation and assess pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Symmetric bilateral reduced striatal uptake with early caudate involvement, pattern atypical for idiopathic PD

optimal_actions.action: Order polysomnography to evaluate for REM sleep behavior disorder and nocturnal stridor
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirmed RBD and nocturnal inspiratory stridor, both supporting MSA diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Consider cardiac MIBG scintigraphy to differentiate PD from MSA
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Preserved cardiac MIBG uptake favoring MSA over PD

optimal_actions.action: Request neuropsychological testing to characterize cognitive profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Frontal-subcortical dysfunction pattern with impaired processing speed and executive functions

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for proposed orthostatic hypotension treatment (midodrine or droxidopa) given current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Midodrine interacts with amlodipine (antagonism) and tamsulosin (alpha-1 antagonism); tamsulosin should be discontinued or switched

optimal_actions.action: Discuss diagnosis of probable MSA, prognosis, and management plan: midodrine or droxidopa for orthostatic hypotension, ENT referral for vocal cord evaluation given stridor, discontinue tamsulosin, hematology referral for MGUS monitoring, and multidisciplinary care

critical_actions: Recognize that symmetric parkinsonism with poor levodopa response and early severe autonomic failure indicates atypical parkinsonism rather than idiopathic PD
Perform formal orthostatic vital signs to quantify neurogenic orthostatic hypotension
Order brain MRI to identify characteristic MSA features (putaminal rim sign, hot cross bun sign, cerebellar atrophy)
Assess levodopa responsiveness with adequate trial or formal challenge
Order autonomic function testing to document severity of multi-domain autonomic failure
Evaluate for nocturnal stridor via polysomnography — stridor is potentially life-threatening in MSA
Do not accept the prior essential tremor diagnosis at face value — re-evaluate tremor characteristics in the context of evolving parkinsonism
Refer to hematology for monoclonal IgA gammopathy monitoring, but recognize this is incidental to the neurological syndrome
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing idiopathic Parkinson's disease without recognizing the red flags for atypical parkinsonism
Escalating levodopa to high doses without recognizing the poor response pattern
Prescribing anticholinergic medications (trihexyphenidyl, benztropine) which would worsen urinary retention, constipation, and orthostatic hypotension
Prescribing dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) which carry high risk of orthostatic hypotension in MSA
Ignoring orthostatic hypotension or attributing it solely to amlodipine and tamsulosin
Failing to evaluate for nocturnal stridor, which is associated with sudden death in MSA
Recommending deep brain stimulation, which is not effective and potentially harmful in MSA
Pursuing bone marrow biopsy or extensive hematologic work-up for the MGUS as a cause of the parkinsonian syndrome — this is a red herring
Starting corticosteroids for presumed polymyalgia rheumatica based on the mildly elevated ESR and muscle complaints — the clinical picture is a neurodegenerative syndrome, not inflammatory
key_reasoning_points: The prior diagnosis of essential tremor creates a cognitive anchoring bias — the change in tremor characteristics (loss of action tremor, emergence of re-emergent postural tremor) suggests the original diagnosis may have been incorrect or a separate process has supervened
Symmetric onset of parkinsonism without classic resting tremor is a red flag against idiopathic PD, which typically presents with asymmetric rest tremor
Poor levodopa response (<30% improvement at supratherapeutic dose) is a cardinal feature distinguishing atypical parkinsonism from PD
Early and severe multi-domain autonomic failure (orthostatic hypotension, urogenital dysfunction, constipation) occurring in parallel with motor onset strongly suggests MSA

The mildly elevated ESR, CRP, weight loss, fatigue, and proximal weakness could suggest polymyalgia rheumatica — this is a red herring; the neurodegenerative syndrome with characteristic imaging explains the clinical picture

The monoclonal IgA gammopathy is an incidental age-related finding (MGUS) that could distract from the primary neurological diagnosis — inspired by the source case where IgA gammopathy was also present
The combination of cerebellar atrophy, pontine hot cross bun sign, and putaminal rim sign on MRI constitutes a characteristic MSA imaging signature
Preserved cardiac MIBG uptake differentiates MSA (preganglionic autonomic failure) from PD (postganglionic cardiac denervation)
Nocturnal stridor is a hallmark of MSA due to vocal cord abductor paresis and is associated with sudden death, requiring urgent ENT evaluation
The family history of essential tremor in father and brother is a red herring that could reinforce the incorrect essential tremor diagnosis
Early postural instability with falls within the first year of motor symptoms is a red flag against typical PD per MDS clinical diagnostic criteria
Propranolol use could theoretically be considered a drug-related contributor to bradykinesia, but the severity of the syndrome and structural MRI changes rule this out as the primary etiology

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle featuring MSA masquerading as PD in an elderly man with prior essential tremor diagnosis, multiple red herrings including monoclonal IgA gammopathy (from source case), mildly elevated inflammatory markers suggesting polymyalgia rheumatica, family history of essential tremor, and medications that could partly explain orthostatic symptoms. The correct diagnosis requires integrating symmetric parkinsonism, poor levodopa response, severe autonomic failure, characteristic MRI findings, and sleep study abnormalities to distinguish MSA from idiopathic PD.
metadata.seed_case_elements_used: Adapted from source case of 76-year-old man with hypertension, coronary disease, peripheral arterial disease, essential tremor, weight loss, fatigue, proximal muscle weakness, elevated ESR, monoclonal IgA gammopathy, and orthostatic symptoms. Clinical elements were transformed from infectious meningitis to neurodegenerative parkinsonian syndrome while preserving the diagnostic complexity, red herrings (IgA gammopathy, inflammatory markers), and atypical presentation features.
